definition: A grimoire is a book of spells — the working manual of ritual magic, listing the names, seals, and rites for summoning and commanding spirits.
history: A French deformation of *grammaire*, 'grammar' — when learning meant Latin and looked, to the unlettered, like sorcery, the grammar-book became the spell-book. The surviving grimoires (the *Key of Solomon*, the *Lesser Key*, the *Ars Notoria*) are working manuals of names, seals, and rites. See the full Word History.

def_sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
def_sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

def_sources.label: Joseph Peterson (ed.), grimoire editions
def_sources.abstract: Not one single book, but Joseph Peterson's long-running project (the "Twilit Grotto: Esoteric Archives" site and related small-press editions) of transcribing and translating historic "grimoires" -- old handbooks of spells, symbols, and spirit-summoning instructions -- and making them freely readable online.

commentary: Older accounts treated the grimoire as a dead medieval relic — a curiosity of Solomonic seals surviving in a few manuscripts. Owen Davies's *Grimoires: A History* recast it as a living, mass-market, and global tradition: cheap print, colonial diffusion, and now the internet kept the spell-book continuously in circulation. Frank Klaassen sharpens the point from the other end, showing that the *printed* grimoire is largely a post-seventeenth-century formation — the *Grimoire du Pape Honorius* stitching medieval scraps into a new book. The field's turn is to read grimoires as *book history* rather than as occult oddities.

commentary_sources.label: Owen Davies, Grimoires: A History of Magic Books
commentary_sources.abstract: Davies traces the history of "grimoires," handbooks of magic spells, charms, and rituals, from the ancient Middle East to modern America. He shows that these books, often imagined as purely forbidden or sinister, actually helped spread literacy, religious ideas, and even early science over two thousand years.

commentary_sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
commentary_sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

etymology.origin: French grimoire, an alteration of grammaire ‘grammar’
etymology.note: A grimoire is, etymologically, just a ‘grammar’ — a book of learning. When most learning was Latin and mysterious, the grammar-book became the spell-book.
etymology.hook: Grammar and grimoire are the same word.

essay.title: Grimoire: the spell-book that was only ever a grammar
essay.dek: How a word for a Latin textbook became the name of Europe's forbidden books of magic.
essay.html:
The etymology is almost too neat to be true, and yet it is: grimoire is a French deformation of grammaire, 'grammar' — Greek grammatikē, 'the art of letters,' by way of Latin. In an age when literacy was rare and Latin was the shared tongue of the Church and of learned magic alike, a book of arcane learning and a book of grammar looked like the same dangerous thing. 'Grammar' grew a shadow-sense of occult knowledge, and the mispronounced grammar-book became the spell-book. (Its Scots cousin, along the very same path, became glamour.)

But a grimoire was never merely a metaphor; it was a real and specific kind of object, and Ted's medieval-magic library is full of them. Frank Klaassen, in The Transformations of Magic, distinguishes the two great streams of late-medieval learned magic — the image-and-astrology magic descending from the Arabic Picatrix, and the ritual 'necromancy' of conjuring texts — and shows how both were copied, hidden, and recopied in exactly these books. The RenaissanceMagicDB catalogues their contents as technical terms: the pentaculum, the exorcismus, the ligatura or binding, the characteres inscribed to compel a spirit.

The surviving grimoires named in the corpus read like the game's own flavor text made real: Joseph Peterson's editions of the Grimorium Verum and the Lesser Key of Solomon; the Ars Notoria, a grimoire that promised, like an MTG card, the instant infusion of knowledge; the conjuring manuals catalogued by Klaassen and by Sophie Page and Catherine Rider in The Routledge History of Medieval Magic. Even John Dee's angelic project, as Deborah Harkness shows in John Dee's Conversations with Angels, was in the end the making of a very learned grammar of the language of angels.

So the word remembers its own history exactly. A grimoire is a grammar — a book that teaches you to read a hidden language and, in reading it aloud, to act. The suspicion fossilised in the word is the whole medieval anxiety about who gets to hold a book.

sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic: Illicit Learned Magic in the Later Middle Ages and Renaissance
sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

sources.label: Joseph H. Peterson (ed.), Grimorium Verum
sources.abstract: Peterson's edition and translation of a notorious 18th-century French handbook of magic that openly gives instructions for calling on demons — one of the few such books from its era that doesn't dress up its subject matter in euphemism.

sources.label: Joseph H. Peterson (ed.), The Lesser Key of Solomon
sources.abstract: A modern scholarly edition of a 17th-century handbook of magic (called a "grimoire") that lists methods and diagrams for summoning and commanding spirits, put together and corrected by Peterson from several old manuscript copies held in the British Library.

sources.label: Sophie Page & Catherine Rider (eds.), The Routledge History of Medieval Magic
sources.abstract: A large reference collection that rounds up current scholarship on magic in medieval Europe (roughly 1100 to 1500), covering the texts people used, how the church and universities reacted, and how magic touched gender roles, art, medicine, and the early witch trials.

sources.label: Deborah E. Harkness, John Dee's Conversations with Angels
sources.abstract: Explores why the Elizabethan scholar John Dee spent his later years trying to talk to angels through a psychic medium, arguing these sessions grew naturally out of his earlier scientific and religious interests rather than being a sign he had lost his mind.

sources.label: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.vv. pentaculum, exorcismus, ligatura, characteres
Etymonline / OED, s.v. grimoire (‹ grammaire ‹ grammar)

flavor_snippets.html: For sale: Complete wizard's library. Rare scrolls, <mark>grimoires</mark>, and works of ancient spellcrafting! Slightly abridged.

". . . And at his passing, the bodies of the world's great warriors shall rise from their graves and follow him to battle."
—The Anvilonian <mark>Grimoire</mark>

mechanics_snippets.html: When Marina Vendrell's <mark>Grimoire</mark> enters, if you cast it, draw five cards.
